shared_groups: Data Return Deletion And Post-Termination Handling
results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to return or delete all personal data to the controller after the end of services. The contract requires the processor, upon expiry of the agreement, to delete all of the controller’s data including personal data, and also gives the controller access to export/return data during the agreement. The post-termination deletion obligation directly overlaps with the regulation’s deletion alternative; the controller-access/export language is additional and does not undermine the required duty.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation triggers after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; the contract triggers upon expiry of the agreement, which is substantially the same end-of-services condition.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses 'will delete,' which is mandatory and at least as strong as 'shall delete/return.'
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract covers all of Controller’s data including Personal Data, which is at least as broad as all personal data. The carve-out for EU or Member State law requiring storage is a permissible legal-retention exception and does not make the obligation weaker than the regulation.
results.contract_source_text: Processor will give Controller access to system functionality in order for the Controller to delete and/or return (i.e. export) any and all of Controller’s data including the Personal Data during the Agreement. Upon expiry of the Agreement the Processor will delete all of Controller’s data including the Personal Data, unless EU or national Member State law requires storage of the Personal Data.

results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

results.contract_source_text: In the event that Processor engages Sub-processors for carrying out Processing activities on behalf of the Controller in accordance with Appendix 5, the same data protection obligations as set out in this Agreement shall be imposed on that Sub-processor by way of a contract or other legal act under EU or national Member State law, in particular providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of this Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to allow for and contribute to audits/inspections by the controller or its mandated auditor, while the contract only mentions preparing an annual audit report and any additional audit reporting/documentation requested by the controller, not allowing or contributing to audits or inspections.

results.contract_source_text: If the Controller requests the Processor to document its Sub-Processor’s compliance with the obligations laid down in Article 28 of the GDPR the Processor shall be entitled to fulfil this obligation by referring Controller to the relevant audit reports or similar documentation made available by the respective Sub-Processors with information indicating that the Sub-Processor in question complies with the GDPR, provided that such audit report(s) or similar documentation shall be based on applicable, acknowledged audit standards, e.g. ISAE 3000 or 3402, ISO 27001 or similar standards. Any additional audit reporting or additional other similar documentation requested by the Controller will be at the Controller’s cost and expenses and is charged under the conditions set out in Appendix 3. 11. results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both address Article 32(1) security measures for processing; the contract requires the controller and processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure security, including encryption and confidentiality/integrity/availability/resilience of systems and services, which matches the regulation's listed measures.

results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.contract_source_text: 'Sub-processor' shall mean any processor engaged by the Processor for the Processing of Personal Data on behalf of the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to ensure that any natural person acting under its authority with access to personal data processes it only on the controller's instructions. The contract clause instead authorizes the processor to transfer/provide data to third-party integration providers and to process data received from them in connection with optional third-party integrations. It does not impose the instruction-only restriction on persons acting under the processor's authority.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the contract is conditional on the Controller choosing to use third-party integration services/applications, while the regulation applies generally to any natural person acting under the processor's authority with access to personal data.
results.modality_check: Weaker/misaligned: the clause is an authorization given by the Controller to the Processor, not an obligation on the Processor to ensure instruction-only processing by its personnel.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract concerns third-party integrations and data transfers/processing in that context, not the manner constraint that persons under the processor's authority may process personal data only on controller instructions.
results.contract_source_text: As part of the Main Agreement the Controller may also choose to use third party integration services and/or applications made available by the Processor on Processor.com in cooperation with the third party providers of such integration services and applications. If the Controller uses such third party integration services and/or applications it hereby authorises the Processor to: i) provide, transmit or transfer the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller to the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application, provided and only to the extent this is necessary for the performance and use by Controller of the said integration services and/or applications, and ii) Process data, including Personal Data, of the Controller that are transferred from the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application to the Controller’s Services with Processor.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to assist the controller in ensuring security of processing, while the contract clause requires flow-down of data protection obligations to sub-processors and sufficient guarantees for technical and organisational measures; it does not impose an assistance duty to the controller.
results.condition_check: The contract is conditional on engaging sub-processors, which is unrelated to the regulation's general assistance duty.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory language for sub-processor obligations, but not for assisting the controller with security.
results.other_constraints_check: The subject matter overlaps with security/technical and organisational measures, but the clause addresses sub-processing governance rather than assistance to the controller.
results.contract_source_text: In the event that Processor engages Sub-processors for carrying out Processing activities on behalf of the Controller in accordance with Appendix 5, the same data protection obligations as set out in this Agreement shall be imposed on that Sub-processor by way of a contract or other legal act under EU or national Member State law, in particular providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of this Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the DPA itself to contain the nature and purpose of the processing. The contract clause addresses the same content by stating that the details of processing, including the purposes for which the data are processed, are specified in Appendix 1, but this is a pointer to another document rather than the content being stated in the clause itself.
results.condition_check: No special condition in the regulation; the contract does not add a conflicting condition, but it defers the content elsewhere.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a declarative specification rather than a weaker permission; modality is not the issue.
results.other_constraints_check: The requirement is a content-in-the-DPA requirement. Because the clause sends the relevant details to Appendix 1 instead of containing them in the clause itself, the locus/content requirement is not satisfied here.
results.contract_source_text: The details of the Processing of Personal Data, and in particular the categories of Data Subjects, types of Personal Data and the purposes for which they are Processed, are specified in Appendix 1, which forms an integral part of the Agreement. 3. Security measures

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both address the processor's use/engagement of a sub-processor and require controller authorization/approval before doing so.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation requires prior specific or general written authorization; the contract says the processor shall not engage a sub-processor unless approved by the Controller, which is at least as restrictive, though the sub-item text alone is incomplete.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The parent clause is mandatory ('shall not'), which is equal-or-greater force than the regulation's prohibition.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The timing/condition is prior approval/authorization, matching the regulation's prior written authorization requirement; the sub-item phrase 'specific instruction from the Controller' does not weaken this when read with the parent context.
results.contract_source_text: specific instruction from the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The contract sub-item and parent context address the processor/controller implementing appropriate technical and organisational security measures, including a process for testing, assessing and evaluating their effectiveness, which matches Art. 32(1)(d).
results.condition_check: Compatible. Both are framed as security measures to be implemented taking account of processing risk; the contract parent clause adds risk-based context but does not weaken the requirement.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses mandatory 'shall implement' / required measures, which meets the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract includes the regular testing/assessment/evaluation process and the broader security-measures context; no looser timing or scope is introduced.
results.contract_source_text: a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing. 2. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by Processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Personal Data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. 3. The Processor hosts the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller via Microsoft Cloud Azure. The online hosting Services are delivered from data centres solely situated within EU, in Ireland and the Netherlands. 4. Particular security measures to be taken out by the Processor under this Agreement are specified in detail on www.Processor.com (Processor's Security and Privacy Protection Policies). results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to ensure that any natural person acting under its authority with access to personal data processes them only on the controller's instructions. results.condition_check: The regulation applies whenever a natural person acting under the processor's authority has access to personal data. The contract is conditional on the controller choosing to use third-party integration services/applications, which is a different situation.
results.modality_check: The regulation is a mandatory processor duty. The contract language is an authorization/permission tied to controller choice, not an obligation that personnel only act on controller instructions.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation concerns manner of processing by natural persons under the processor's authority. The contract concerns data transfer and processing for third-party integrations, not that access/use is limited to controller instructions.

results.core_alignment_check: processor/controller: the regulation requires processor assistance to the controller specifically for DPIA compliance, while the contract requires the processor to implement technical and organisational measures to meet GDPR and protect data subjects; this is a general security/compliance duty, not DPIA assistance

results.modality_check: Both are mandatory/obligatory in force, but the contract obligation is of a different kind
results.other_constraints_check: The contract's manner/target is general GDPR compliance and rights protection, not the specific assistance with DPIA compliance required by the regulation
results.contract_source_text: The Processor agrees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing of the Personal Data will meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable national and EU data protection law and ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the DPA to state the types of personal data processed, while the contract sub-item names categories of data subjects (controller’s employees, board members and officers), which is the wrong axis.

results.modality_check: The parent clause is mandatory in form, but modality does not cure the axis mismatch.

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller; the contract instead authorizes the processor to transfer/provide data to third-party providers and to process data transferred from those providers in connection with optional integration services. It does not state that processing is done on documented controller instructions, nor does it impose that instruction-based limitation.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the contract is conditioned on the Controller choosing to use third-party integration services/applications, which is a different trigger from documented instructions from the controller.
results.modality_check: Weaker/misaligned: the contract uses an authorization tied to a specific optional use case, not a mandatory obligation to process only on documented instructions.
results.other_constraints_check: Different object and scope: it concerns third-party integration services, data transfers to providers, and processing of data transferred from providers, rather than the general processing of personal data under documented controller instructions.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures to ensure an appropriate level of security. The contract sub-item only refers to a general description of such measures, and moreover points to Appendix 2 and later amendments rather than itself imposing or describing the measures here. The parent clause is about records of processing activities, not security implementation.
results.condition_check: Compatible in the abstract, but the clause does not itself set any triggering condition for implementing security measures.
results.modality_check: Weaker / non-obligatory as presented: the sub-item is a descriptive record entry, not a mandatory duty to implement security measures.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation's risk-based appropriateness requirement is not substantively delivered by a mere description of measures in an appendix reference.
results.contract_source_text: a general description of the technical and organisational security measures as referred to in Appendix 2 and subsequent amendments thereto.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures for personal data processing; the contract obligates the Processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures so that processing meets GDPR requirements and protects data subjects' rights, which substantively covers the same security/organizational safeguard obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation speaks generally of measures to ensure security; the contract imposes the measures without a weaker or conflicting condition.
results.modality_check: Compatible. 'Agrees to implement' is a binding obligation, equal to or stronger than the regulatory 'can include' list of measures.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract's scope is at least as broad and not contradictory; it targets processing of personal data and protection of rights, which encompasses the security objective, though it does not enumerate each example measure.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor agrees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing of the Personal Data will meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable national and EU data protection law and ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the DPA to state the categories of data subjects (classes of persons). The contract parent clause states categories of data, and the sub-item names 'Sensitive data (cf. GDPR article 9)', which is a type of personal data, not a category of data subjects. Wrong axis, so no substantive overlap.
results.condition_check: No specific triggering condition in the regulation or contract beyond inclusion in the DPA; not applicable.
results.modality_check: The parent clause is mandatory in context, but modality does not cure the axis mismatch.
results.other_constraints_check: No timing/manner/location constraints relevant; the issue is content axis mismatch and the sub-item is not the required subject category.
results.contract_source_text: Sensitive data (cf. GDPR article 9)*.

Same obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both apply when assessing the appropriate level of security / level of security.
results.modality_check: Compatible: contract uses mandatory 'shall be taken into account', matching or exceeding the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the listed risks and data states (transmitted, stored, otherwise processed) substantially match the regulation's risks and scope.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation concerns a processor's ability to process personal data without documented instructions when required by Union or Member State law. The contract clause instead requires processing in accordance with security measures in Appendix 2, which is a different obligation about security compliance, not legal-authority-based processing without instructions.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the regulation's trigger is requirement by Union or Member State law; the contract has no such condition.
results.modality_check: Not aligned: the regulation permits processing in that circumstance; the contract imposes a mandatory security-compliance duty.
results.other_constraints_check: Different object and locus: security measures in an appendix versus processing without documented instructions under law.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to contractually impose the Article 28(3) GDPR obligations on engaged sub-processors, while the contract clause only covers prior notice and objection/approval mechanics for adding or replacing sub-processors.
results.condition_check: The regulation is unconditional as to imposing obligations on sub-processors; the contract clause is conditioned on general written authorisation and change notifications, which is a different situation.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory language for notification and deemed acceptance, but it does not impose the required substantive obligations on sub-processors.
results.other_constraints_check: The subject matter overlaps only at the level of sub-processors, but the required target obligation (flow-down of Article 28(3) terms by contract or legal act) is absent.
results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Processor obligation to notify controller of sub-processor changes and obtain/allow objection; regulation requires processor to bind engaged sub-processors by contract or other legal act to the same Article 28(3) obligations. These are different obligations.
results.condition_check: The contract is conditioned on general written authorisation and change notifications, not on imposing Article 28(3) obligations on sub-processors.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory notification/objection mechanics, but it does not impose the required contractual flow-down obligation on sub-processors.
results.other_constraints_check: No equivalent requirement to contractually bind sub-processors to the same GDPR obligations; the subject matter is sub-processor change approval, not downstream contractual imposition.

However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to impose Article 28(3)-equivalent obligations on engaged sub-processors by contract or other legal act. The contract clause instead permits the processor to engage sub-processors for consultancy services; it does not require passing down GDPR processor obligations to them.

results.other_constraints_check: The contract addresses engagement of sub-processors for services, not the specific manner of imposing the same obligations by contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to assist the controller specifically in consulting supervisory authorities before processing in high-risk cases. The contract instead imposes a general security/compliance obligation to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to meet GDPR requirements and protect data subjects. That is a different duty, not assistance with prior consultation.
results.condition_check: The regulation is triggered where processing would result in a high risk absent controller mitigation measures. The contract has no comparable consultation/high-risk trigger.

results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.condition_check: Compatible. Both are conditioned on risk/state of the art/nature, scope, context and purposes of processing; the contract is at least as specific and risk-based.

results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

Notwithstanding the before mentioned, Processor is not entitled to remuneration for circumstances which are attributable to Processor’s breach of security or Processor’s breach of it’s obligations as set out in this Agreement.

In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by Processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Personal Data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. 3. The Processor hosts the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller via Microsoft Cloud Azure. The online hosting Services are delivered from data centres solely situated within EU, in Ireland and the Netherlands. 4. Microsoft Azure platform provides the security measures as described on www.Processor.com. 5. results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to ensure that natural persons acting under its authority with access to personal data process it only on controller instructions; the contract instead addresses compliance with legislative changes and change-of-instructions/termination mechanics, not staff access or instruction-limited processing.

results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. However, the Processor shall not be obligated to implement any change of the instructions if the Parties cannot in good faith agree to reasonable terms for the implementation. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

results.modality_check: The contract uses definitional language, not an obligation to include the required information.
results.other_constraints_check: No timing/manner/location content relevant to the required processing nature and purpose is provided.
results.contract_source_text: ‘Agreement’ shall mean this data processing agreement including its appendices;

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures appropriate to risk. The contract only authorizes processing on instructions and permits use of means like software, servers, and IT systems; it does not impose a security-measures obligation or a risk-based security standard.
results.condition_check: The regulation’s trigger is the need for security appropriate to risk; the contract has no comparable risk-based security condition for security measures.
results.modality_check: The contract’s relevant language is permissive ('may') regarding means and mandatory only for processing on instructions, not for implementing security measures.
results.other_constraints_check: No equivalent requirement to implement technical and organisational measures, ensure security, or calibrate measures to varying likelihood and severity is stated; the clause is about processing authority and means of processing.
results.contract_source_text: The Controller hereby authorises the Processor to Process the Personal Data on behalf of the Controller on the terms and conditions set out in this Agreement. The Processor shall Process the Personal Data only on documented instructions from the Controller. The Parties agree that this Agreement shall constitute the instructions as of the date of the Agreement. The Processor may – unless otherwise specifically is set out in the Agreement – apply all relevant means, including software, servers and IT systems.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to inform the controller of intended sub-processor changes and give the controller a right to object; the contract instead says the processor remains liable for its sub-processor's obligations. Different obligation.
results.condition_check: Not applicable / no matching condition. The contract does not address notice of intended changes or objection rights.
results.modality_check: Not applicable / no matching modality. The contract imposes liability, not the required notice-and-objection framework.
results.other_constraints_check: Not applicable / no matching timing, target, or location constraints.
results.contract_source_text: Processor shall remain liable to the Controller for the performance of its Sub- processor's obligations.

shared_groups: Personal Data Breach Notification And Content
results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the content of a personal data breach notification to include specific information (nature of breach, DPO contact details, consequences, mitigation measures). The contract instead imposes a controller-to-processor notice duty about possible unauthorized use of login information/passwords/credentials or other security breaches, which is a different obligation and does not state the required notification contents.
results.condition_check: The regulation applies upon notification of a personal data breach; the contract triggers on becoming aware of any possible unauthorized use of credentials or other security breaches. This is related but not the same condition and is framed as an internal notice to the processor rather than the Article 33(3) breach-notification content requirement.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'shall immediately notify,' which is mandatory and at least as strong as the regulation's obligation, but modality does not cure the mismatch in obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds timing ('immediately'), recipient (the Processor), and form ('in writing'), but it omits the required notification contents and addresses a different recipient and duty. The extra constraints do not satisfy the regulation's substantive content requirement.
results.contract_source_text: The Controller shall immediately notify the Processor in writing after becoming aware of any possible unauthorised use of log-in information, passwords, credentials or other security breaches related to the Main Agreement. 7. Records of Processing activities

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation gives the controller a right to suspend processing in certain cases, while the contract only gives the controller an opportunity to object to changes in sub-processors and deems changes accepted if no objection is made; it does not confer a suspension right.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the regulation refers to suspension in certain cases, but the contract concerns notice/objecting to sub-processor changes under general written authorisation and a 30-day non-objection rule.
results.modality_check: Weaker/mismatched: the contract is about notice and objection mechanics, not an equal-or-greater obligation to allow suspension.
results.other_constraints_check: Different subject matter and remedy: sub-processor change approval versus suspension of processing; no equivalent timing/location/manner issue bridges that gap.
results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to assist the controller with DPIA compliance, while the contract sub-item only concerns describing the nature of a personal data breach and reasonable mitigation measures; different obligation and different subject matter.
results.condition_check: No matching DPIA-triggering condition; the contract text is about breach notification content.
results.modality_check: Not applicable to the required DPIA assistance duty because the contract does not impose that duty.
results.other_constraints_check: No relevant overlap on timing/manner/target/location for DPIA assistance; the clause is a content item within breach notification.
results.contract_source_text: reasonable measures taken or proposed by the Processor to mitigate the consequences of the Personal Data Breach.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to inform the controller immediately if an instruction infringes GDPR/data protection law; the contract clause instead concerns optional use of third-party integration services and authorisation to transfer/process data.
results.condition_check: No matching triggering condition; the contract is conditioned on the controller choosing to use third-party integrations, not on an unlawful instruction.
results.modality_check: The contract is permissive/authorising, not an obligation to notify.
results.other_constraints_check: No equivalent immediacy, notification, or infringement-reporting duty appears.
results.contract_source_text: As part of the Main Agreement the Controller may also choose to use third party integration services and/or applications made available by the Processor on Processor.com in cooperation with the third party providers of such integration services and applications. If the Controller uses such third party integration services and/or applications it hereby authorises the Processor to: i) provide, transmit or transfer the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller to the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application, provided and only to the extent this is necessary for the performance and use by Controller of the said integration services and/or applications, and ii) Process data, including Personal Data, of the Controller that are transferred from the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application to the Controller’s Services with Processor.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the contract to substantively provide security measures (or equivalent obligations) such as pseudonymisation, confidentiality/integrity/availability/resilience, timely restoration, and regular testing/evaluation. The contract clause only says the details of the security measures are specified in Appendix 2; it points elsewhere and does not itself state the measures.
results.condition_check: No specific triggering condition is at issue beyond the general security-measures requirement; the clause does not supply the required substantive conditions or measures here.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes substantive security obligations; the contract clause is merely a pointer to an appendix and does not impose the measures in this text.
results.other_constraints_check: No compatible timing, manner, or target details are provided in the clause itself; it defers all such substance to Appendix 2.
results.contract_source_text: The details of the security measures taken by Processor in this respect of the Processing of Personal Data, are specified in Appendix 2, which forms an integral part of the Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to assist the controller in communicating a personal data breach to the data subject. The contract text only defines 'Processor’s Services' as support and/or education services provided by the Processor to the Controller; it does not mention personal data breaches, communication to data subjects, or assistance with that task.
results.condition_check: No relevant condition is stated in the contract text.
results.modality_check: No operative obligation is stated; this is definitional language, not a mandatory assistance duty.
results.other_constraints_check: No timing, manner, target, or location constraints relevant to breach communication are provided.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the parent clause introduces categories of data processed, and the sub-item names a concrete type of personal data ('Sensitive data' / special category data), which is the same obligation to state the types of personal data.
results.condition_check: No explicit condition beyond the parent's 'may fall within any of the following categories'; this is compatible with a requirement to contain the types of personal data.
results.modality_check: Mandatory in context: the parent clause is a required list of categories, and the sub-item is part of that list, so it satisfies the 'shall contain' requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: The requirement is a content requirement for types of personal data; the clause provides an in-line named type/category, not a pointer elsewhere. Axis matches.
results.contract_source_text: Sensitive data (cf. GDPR article 9)*.

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to impose GDPR Article 28(3)-equivalent obligations on engaged sub-processors by contract or other legal act; the contract text only says Microsoft is a sub-processor providing hosting services and describes data hosting location, but it does not state that Microsoft is bound by the same Article 28(3)-type obligations.
results.condition_check: No specific triggering condition beyond engaging sub-processors; the clause mentions an agreement with Microsoft but not the required substantive condition of imposing equivalent obligations.
results.modality_check: The regulation uses a mandatory 'shall'; the contract text is descriptive and does not impose the required obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The required manner is by contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law; while the text mentions an agreement, it does not specify that the agreement imposes the same obligations, and the hosting-location statements are unrelated.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor has entered into an agreement with Microsoft Corporation whereby Microsoft is a Sub-processor to the Processor acting (on the Controller's behalf) to provide hosting services via the Microsoft Azure Cloud Platform. All of Controller's data including Personal Data are hosted on the Microsoft Azure Cloud Platform in Ireland and the Netherlands. The hosting services do not take place in countries outside EU.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to assess security by taking into account risks of accidental/unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure, or access to personal data. That is a different obligation, not security-risk assessment.

results.modality_check: The contract text is descriptive/content-like, but modality is irrelevant because the underlying obligation is different.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the DPA to contain the categories of data subjects, while the contract sub-item provides a general description of technical and organisational security measures (and points to Appendix 2), which is a different content axis.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller. The contract clause instead addresses compliance with legislative changes, implementation of changes to instructions, and termination if the parties cannot agree on terms; it does not itself impose the processor's duty to process personal data on documented controller instructions.
results.condition_check: The regulation's condition is that processing occurs on documented instructions from the controller. The contract's condition concerns inability to agree on reasonable terms for changes to instructions and GDPR-driven legislative changes, which is a different situation.
results.modality_check: The contract uses obligations about reasonable endeavours and termination rights, but not an obligation matching the processor's duty to process personal data on documented instructions.

results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. However, the Processor shall not be obligated to implement any change of the instructions if the Parties cannot in good faith agree to reasonable terms for the implementation. If the Parties fail to agree in good faith to reasonable terms regarding change of the instructions each Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement with a written notice of 60 days, provided that such changes are deemed necessary to comply with the GDPR or other applicable EU or national data protection laws and regulation. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires regular testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational security measures, and the contract expressly provides for 'a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing.'

results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract states a required 'process' within the agreement, which is mandatory in context and at least as strong as the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the subject matter, scope, and purpose of the testing/evaluation duty match the regulation's security-measures requirement; no looser timing, target, or location constraint appears.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to immediately inform the controller when an instruction infringes GDPR or other data protection provisions. results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation gives the controller a right to terminate the DPA in certain cases; the contract gives either party a right to terminate the Main Agreement for the other party’s material breach, with a cure period. That is a different object (Main Agreement vs DPA) and a different trigger (material breach vs unspecified certain cases for the controller).
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the contract conditions are limited to material breach and cure, while the regulation does not specify breach and is broader/unspecified.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'may' (a permission/right), which is compatible with a right to terminate, but this does not cure the mismatch in object and trigger.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract is narrower and differently targeted: termination of the Main Agreement, only for breach, and only after notice/cure where remediable. The regulation concerns termination of the DPA in certain cases.
results.contract_source_text: A Party may terminate the Main Agreement in the event of the other Party’s material breach of this Agreement. Where such breach is capable of being remedied, a Party may only terminate the Main Agreement if the breaching Party has not remedied such breach within 30 days after giving written notice of such breach and the consequences of failure to remedy the breach.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the DPIA itself to include specified substantive elements; the contract requires the processor to assist the controller with carrying out a DPIA and prior consultation. results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller in its compliance with an obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) and prior consulting of supervisory authorities where required, cf. articles 35 and 36 of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation allocates processor liability for damage caused by processing only in two processor-related cases: non-compliance with GDPR obligations directed to processors, or acting outside/contrary to the controller's lawful instructions. The contract's scope is lawful basis for transfers to third-party providers and processing by those providers, which is a different target and subject matter.

results.contract_source_text: ‘Personal Data’ shall mean any information Processed by Processor in connection with the provision of the Processors’ Services under this Agreement relating to an identified or identifiable natural person ('Data Subject'); an identifiable person is one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data, an online identifier or to one or more factors specific to his physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that person;

results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires appropriate technical and organisational security measures, including encryption/pseudonymisation, confidentiality/integrity/availability/resilience of systems and services, timely restoration of availability/access, and regular testing/evaluation. The contract parent clause obliges the Controller and Processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, and specifically includes encryption of personal data and the ability to ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services. This directly overlaps with the Art. 32(1) security-measures obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible. Both are conditioned on risk/state of the art/nature, scope, context and purposes of processing; the contract is at least as specific and risk-based as the regulation.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses 'shall implement', which is mandatory and at least as strong as the regulation's requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible for the overlapping parts. The contract's measures are framed as appropriate to the risk and include encryption and system/service resilience/confidentiality/integrity/availability, which matches the regulation's security-measure content. The regulation's additional examples of timely restoration and regular testing/evaluation are not expressly listed, but the clause still covers the same core security-measures obligation and is not weaker on the measures it does include.

results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.other_constraints_check: The clause is about sub-processing and hosting services, not the required identity-and-contact-details content.

results.core_alignment_check: processor/controller instruction compliance: the regulation requires the processor to process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, while the contract clause is about complying with legislative changes and possible termination if instruction changes cannot be agreed; it does not state that processing is done on documented controller instructions
results.condition_check: regulation has no special triggering condition beyond documented controller instructions; contract instead conditions on legislative changes and inability to agree on reasonable terms for changes to instructions
results.modality_check: the contract uses reasonable endeavours and a termination right, but does not impose the required obligation to process only on documented instructions
results.other_constraints_check: no matching timing/manner/location constraint for documented instructions; the clause addresses change-management and termination, not instruction-based processing
results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. However, the Processor shall not be obligated to implement any change of the instructions if the Parties cannot in good faith agree to reasonable terms for the implementation. If the Parties fail to agree in good faith to reasonable terms regarding change of the instructions each Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement with a written notice of 60 days, provided that such changes are deemed necessary to comply with the GDPR or other applicable EU or national data protection laws and regulation. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the DPA to state the nature and purpose of the processing. The contract text instead discusses security measures, hosting location, and assistance/audit costs; it does not itself state the nature and purpose of the processing.
results.condition_check: No relevant condition overlap; the requirement is unconditional and the contract does not supply the required content.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory language for other matters, but not for the required nature-and-purpose content.
results.other_constraints_check: No matching content on the required subject matter; references to external websites are pointers, not the actual nature/purpose statement.

results.contract_source_text: As part of the Main Agreement the Controller may also choose to use third party integration services and/or applications made available by the Processor on Processor.com in cooperation with the third party providers of such integration services and applications. If the Controller uses such third party integration services and/or applications it hereby authorises the Processor to: i) provide, transmit or transfer the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller to the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application, provided and only to the extent this is necessary for the performance and use by Controller of the said integration services and/or applications, and ii) Process data, including Personal Data, of the Controller that are transferred from the third party provider of the relevant integration service and/or application to the Controller’s Services with Processor.

results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;
